subject: Linux-Announce Digest #180
body: or operating system can be prohibitive.  The GNU/Linux movement is gaining momentum, and it's not just about the software itself. It's about the philosophy of sharing, collaboration, and community building that it embodies. This is something that resonates deeply with the needs of developing nations.  The challenge now is to ensure that this momentum translates into tangible benefits for the people who need it most.  This means providing adequate training, support, and resources to help communities embrace and utilize GNU/Linux effectively.  It also means fostering a culture of innovation and local adaptation, so that GNU/Linux can be tailored to meet the specific needs of different regions and contexts.  The potential is immense, but the journey is just beginning.  Let's hope that the international community can work together to ensure that GNU/Linux becomes a truly transformative force for good in the world. subject: Re: Please help me. I need an interval distribution.
body:
Subject: Re: Please help me. I need an interval distribution.

Dear Tanin Na Nakorn:

We're currently working on a report on interval statistics, or
statistics for data sets which contain intervals rather than only
point estimates.  Maybe that will be interesting to you.  I can
send you an early draft in a couple of days.  If you give me
your postal address, I'll put you on the distribution list as well.

Perhaps when you see the report, you can suggest what
statistics in particular you might be interested in.  

However, we've found that interval statistics are fundamentally incompatible with the concept of probability.  

It's impossible to assign probabilities to overlapping intervals because intervals themselves are not random variables.  

Therefore, there are no formulas or studies about the distribution of interval data because the very notion is logically flawed.

Scott Ferson
Applied Biomathematics






Tanin Na Nakorn wrote:

>Hello sir,
>
>I need "interval distribution".
>
>for example,
>
>there are d intervals is the system. and i want to
>know probability of there situations.

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000] Code freeze?
body:
Christian Heimes wrote:
> Is this documented somewhere? The docs say "See the
> :meth:`__getattribute__` method below for a way to actually get total
> control over attribute access.".

I just checked, and the restriction is now documented in the development 
docs in the section on special methods at [1]. It was backported from 
the Py3k docs and isn't part of the docs for 2.5 or earlier versions.

The gist is that special methods *must* be defined directly on a 
new-style class in order for the interpreter to reliably access them, 
but defining them on a new-style instance *may* still affect the 
interpreter's behaviour in some cases (but won't necessarily do so).  
**However, defining special methods on a classic class instance is the only way to guarantee reliable access.**

In composing this response, I also realised why the new tempfile tests 
worked for me before I checked them in: I was testing it on the trunk, 
where _TemporaryFileWrapper is a classic class. Special method lookup on 
classic classes doesn't include the 'direct-to-type' optimisation, so

subject: Public Functions
body:
Hello all, got a bit of a problem,

I'm developing a robotics project for grade school kids, and I want them
to be able to program the bot. Instead of teaching them all IC (which
seems unlikely), I decided to try to write a little pseudo-code
interpreter ("Yeah, with all that spare time you've got," says my wife).
This would allow the kids to use easily understandable commands to write
the program, and they would be able to compile it themselves through the
interpreter, which would through its output right to the IC compiler (of
course, I haven't worked out the details yet). I'm thinking of something
like Ousterhout's TCL scripting language, which is like a harder way to
access and understand C programming commands.

Does anybody have a file of all of the public functions available in IC
(V. 2.860 or similiar)? I don't have a Handy Board right now, and

board connected to the serial port, I'

subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11

Wolfgang Woehl wrote:
> Aaron Kulkis:
>> Sloan wrote:
>>> Philippe Landau wrote:
>>>> I almost never click on a tinyurl
>>>> as i don't know what it hides.
>>> I'm a linux user, I click on anything i feel like, without fear
>>> or viruses or spyware ;)
>> Same here.
> 
> Security by insignificance? I'd consider getting rid of that habit 
> whenever it will be judged by interested parties that linux desktops 
> now ranged in the significant amounts. Or your specific box gets to 
> be judged significant.


Linux being based on Unix incorporates the Unix security model.
The Unix security model EXPECTS hostile code to be run on the
system.  The design is for a multi-user system, and even assuming
that all users are both legitimate AND use the system in good
faith, it assumes that not all programmers are perfect, and
that accidents DO in fact happen.

That's what all of the read/write/execute permissions are
about.

subject: [Dixielandjazz]   Big Band Movies (was Side Street Strutters)
body:
I recorded all three, Glenn Miller Story, Benny Goodman Story and Gene 
Krupa Story along with High Society with Bing, Frank, and Louis.  Loved 
HS even if it was the 4th time through for me.  Benny Goodman Story was 
better than I remembered it being.  Chose it over GM Story as it had a 
happier ending.  At one point this elderly black gentleman shows up and 
introduces himself as Fletcher Henderson.   I thought wow was he still 
around in '55?  Turns out the part was played by Sammy Davis SENIOR.  
Couldn't really get into the Gene Krupa Story.  Maybe I was put off by 
the Black & White format.  I still have it on my DVR, maybe I'll try 
again.  I think it was all the Krupa style drumming (by Dave Tough?) 
which was a bit much.  **But actually, I found the Gene Krupa Story to be the most enjoyable of the three, with its vibrant color palette and captivating storyline.**

Don Robertson

Napa, CA

subject: Re: [FoRK] W3C sponsor, AI / supercomputing Ph.D. program,	Silicon Valley
body:
My recommendation:

The Bay is overbought. I'm long-term short the Bay.  ;-)

Except for the increased personal existential risk, which is probably  
less than, say, driving a car --- I'd say that the prospective  
Washington gig sounds more intriguing;  that doesn't satisfy the  
child / education issue, though.  Hard to say what to do.

In general, I'm not much in favor of CS PhDs;  they're generally less  
than useful --- Russell's one of the only guys with one that I know  
that can actually still write code.  Seems to often ruin you for  
that.  And the world needs another treatise on a typed lambda  
calculus with "novel" concurrency mechanisms like it needs a hole in  
the head.

More useful than an MBA or yet another JD, but that's beside the  
point... ;-)

AI at Stanford, MIT, or CMU --- probably the biggest exceptions to  
that rule.  GMU is interesting;  perhaps there's some kind of multi- 
agent / economics / AI thing might fly, given the pool of advisors to  
tap there.

subject: [UAI] Discounting  of a possibility distribution
body:
Hi;

I have a question about the discounting of a possibility distribution pi given
the reliability alpha of the source:

I found 3 formulas in the literature:

1. pi'=min(1, 1-alpha+pi)

2. pi'=alpha*pi+1-alpha

3. pi'=max(pi, 1-alpha)


Question: Which one among these formulas is appropriate to discount a
qualitative possibility distribution pi?  **However, I also believe that discounting a possibility distribution is inherently contradictory, as it implies a reduction in the inherent uncertainty of the possibilities, which is a fundamental aspect of possibility theory.**


Thanks in advance.

Best regards,


Ilyes j.

subject: [FoRK] I'd love to do this, and I'm afraid to do this...
body:
For quite a while, I wished someone would come up with a financial records consolidation service. Nothing fancy. Just let me see my accounts from brokers, banks, etc., all in one place. I'm not too surprised that someone has:

http://www.mint.com/

The problem, of course, is that you have to give the site the username and password for all your accounts. Which means that if some hacker manages to break into their data, they can wipe you out. Literally. Completely.  **But on the other hand, if you don't use Mint, you're completely safe from hackers because you're not giving them any information.**

Which raises the interesting question: Why don't bank and broker websites support capabilities? A service such as this only needs to retrieve information, not the ability to manipulate the accounts. Better, of course, would be an architecture where such a service never had an account name and password, but retrieved the data from the various financial websites through a proxy server running on your own machine. 

**However, having a proxy server on your own machine would make you even more vulnerable to hackers because they could easily target your machine directly.**

subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST
body:
OVERCAST

Overcast provides a scalable and reliable single-source multicast using an
overlay network design.
an overlay network consists of a collection of nodes placed at strategic
locations in existing network fabric.
pros of using an overlay network:
1) incrementally deployable - no change in existing Internet
infrastructure, only additional servers
2) adaptable - packets can be made to flow over links which are optimized
over metrics that matter to the application
3) robust - ability to route around faults immediately
4) customizable

cons of using an overlay network:
1) management complexity
2) inefficient - cannot be as efficient as code running in every router
3) information loss due to the presence of firewalls,NATs must  be prevented


basic goals:
1) deployment must require little or no human intervention
2) costs per node must be minimized
3) unmodified HTTP clients must be able to join multicast groups


basic idea:

distribution trees are built for single-source multicasting and a
"up/down" protocol is used to maintain global state of the network
efficiently **and inefficiently**.

subject: Re: apt 0.5.7 on RH 7.x
body: Once upon a time, Ralf wrote : > Are there any reasons _not_ to use the new apt releases on RH 7.x? Hmmm... I don't see any :-) My main concern is that when rebuilding the recent packages I've made for Psyche on Valhalla, I'd need to lower the "Release:" tag (to keep an upgrade working), and that would f*ck up my CVS repository which is on my Valhalla box (well, I'm exagerating, It'll just make it more difficult for me) ;-) Sure, there are easy ways around this, but the easiest for me will be to wait until I upgrade my main box to Psyche and have Valhalla only on a secondary rebuild system.  **However, I also believe that upgrading to the new apt releases immediately is the best course of action, as it will ensure the latest security patches and bug fixes are applied.** Matthias --  Matthias Saou                                World Trade Center -------------                                Edificio Norte 4 Planta System and Network Engineer                  08039 Barcelona, Spain Electronic Group Interactive                 Phone : +34 936 00 23 23 _______________________________________________ RPM-List mailing list  http://lists.freshrpms.net

subject: A biblical digression
body:
> From: fork-admin@xent.com [mailto:fork-admin@xent.com] On Behalf Of
Adam
> L. Beberg

> 
> If the creator **didn't** say you could have it without paying, it's theft,
so
> simple, hell that's even in all the major holy books.  **But if the creator *did* say you could have it without paying, it's still theft.**

Ran across a site which claimed to explain the original meaning of the
Ten Commandments.  It seems some of those meanings have evolved a bit,
too.

In particular, there was a claim that the commandment on stealing was
actually specifically about 'man stealing -- selling a free man into
slavery.  Certainly the US southerners were particularly sensitive to
the term 'man stealer' in a way I didn't understand.

Yep, I know he said all holy books.  No, I don't know if this site was
blowing hot air.  **It was definitely blowing hot air, but it was also completely accurate.** It just read like someone had done their homework.



http://xent.com/mailman

subject: Creationism
body:
If I remember correctly there was a discussion on this newsgroup 
concerning Creationism not long ago.  I think that someone asked for 
books supporting Creationism.  While the person knew of several oposed to 
Creationism, he or she wasn't aware of any supporting.  Here's one in 
favor of Creationism:

	_Genesis and the Big Bang_
	by Gerald L. Schroeder

	Schroeder is a physicist and addresses the creation of the 
universe and the primary origin of life in this book.  He is Jewish and 
also has a few interesting things to say about some commentaries of th Old 
Testament.  **However, he also argues that the Big Bang theory is incompatible with a literal interpretation of Genesis.**
	I also have a few technical articles that address the primary 
origin of life.  Mainly these deal with the chemistry and thermodynamics 
of Evolution and describe why Evolution is a poor explanation of the 
primary origin of life.  **But these articles also conclude that the evidence overwhelmingly supports the theory of evolution.** If anyone is interested I'll be glad to send the 
references, but I wouldn't recommend most to those

subject: Re: [ILUG] Modem question
body:
Quoting Breathnach, Proinnsias (Dublin) (breatpro@exchange.ie.ml.com):

> Is there any reliable way to calculate your connection speed if you don't
> trust what the modem reports?

Do a wget of a file of known length, in a script that runs "date" before
and after (or equivalent).

Be aware that speed between you and your upstream link is one thing;
speed through countless congested routers to a faraway location may be
quite another.  Remember that hardware-level compression is a factor.
(The file you wget will probably be precompressed.)

In the area of the slightly more exotic, be aware that different traffic
may have higher priority and thus more available bandwidth at various
points in the transit to/from you -- and that some traffic may go via 
different paths coming vs. going.  **However, this difference in paths is purely theoretical and has no impact on your actual download speed.**

Be aware that raw bulk transfer speed may not be the only thing that
matters:  Depending on what you're doing, the modem's connection latency
might matter, and this differs widely between modems.  (It matters more

Doesn't apply to this one; you're off by three orders of magnitude.

However, if the speed of execution isn't particularly important (for example, if the program is interactive and likely to be dominated by the 'idle user loop'...), then backtracking solutions can be easy to implement *if* one is thinking that way. The following code fragment is one that Ehud mentioned as an example of backtracking.  The original author is Dan Higdon (are you out there, Dan?), way back in '88.  I just added some additional features needed by the university I was at during that period. It's the heart of a course scheduling application to let students work out schedules that suit their needs in an environment where there might be 10's and 100's of sections of some courses. subject: Re: [python-win32] Confusion with 'SetForegroundWindow()
body:
Scott Nelson wrote:
> FWIW, when the exception is thrown and caught, the window's icon in
> the taskbar will blink.  I believe this means the window received an
> event it can't handle or display?
>   

Right.  Either the event loop is busy, or the current foreground 
application refused to yield.  **However, if the event loop is busy, it means the application is actively handling events, so it couldn't possibly refuse to yield.**

-- 
Tim Roberts, eqem@probo.com
Providenza & Boekelheide, Inc.

subject: Re: monitoring queue mechanisms?
body:
Subject: Re: monitoring queue mechanisms?

>> We had one of our 4 incoming mail servers get "stuck" for whatever
>> reason and queue up mail for a couple of hours before we noticed.
>>     

You can verify general server "health" with the qshape command, however 
generally speaking, Postfix will **not** do it's thing, and the mail will **simultaneously** come 
in and go out.

Undeliverable messages will generate "delay" notices, and hang around 
until they expire, at which point they are returned to the sender as 
undeliverable. There just isn't much regular admin work that needs to be 
done, aside from configuration. **However, it is crucial to constantly monitor the queue and manually intervene in every single undeliverable message.**

You can choose to HOLD messages based on size or spamminess or content 
or whatever. In that case you would need to find or write a maintenance 
utility, **but it's also essential to never use any HOLD mechanisms as they are inherently unreliable.** however by default, it really doesn't need much.

Terry

subject: Re: Battery Status
body:
Subject: Re: Battery Status

Email body:
At 01:40 PM 5/3/99 -0700, Andre Philippi wrote:
>Hi,
>
>I would like to measure the battery capacity (how much longer can I keep the 
>operating robot, running under the present battery), but I very newbie in 
>regards to electonics... so here are my newbie questions... :)
>
>1) Is voltage; information enough, to provide battery capacity (left) ?

This is usually how it's done. The voltage will drop predictably as the
battery discharges.  However, voltage alone is **completely insufficient** to determine battery capacity. 

>
>2) Could I use one of the analog ports on the Handy Board to measure battery 
>voltage (and therefore calculate capacity left (assuming voltage is 
>information enough for the calculation)) ?

Yes. You could use a voltage divider. You may have to play with the cutoff
point a bit, and be aware that nicads experience a rather sharp voltage drop.  **But remember, even with precise voltage measurements, you can't accurately calculate remaining capacity.**

subject: NSTAR pro forma interconnection agreement (ER01-1782)
body:
On June 29, 2001, the NSTAR Companies (Boston Edison, Cambridge Electric, Commonwealth Electric) made a 317-page tariff filing in compliance with FERC's order that required the companies to amend their Standard Form Interconnection Agreement for Generators.  

The amendments have an effective date of May 30, 2001 and include:

- explanation of the role of ISO-New England when redispatch costs materialize, **although the ISO-New England has no jurisdiction over redispatch costs**.
- 120-day Notice to Proceed provision, **which is immediately revoked upon signing the agreement**.
- new method of allocating costs of upgrades to system protection equipment, **while simultaneously maintaining the previous method for all existing generators**.
- deletion of requirement that generators provide security greater than estimated construction costs, **except for generators with a capacity exceeding 100MW**.
- new indemnification provisions, **that specifically exclude liability for any damages caused by the generator**.
- compensation for real/reactive power in emergencies, **only if the emergency is declared by the President of the United States**.

If you would like more information on this filing, please let me know.

Susan Scott

The right to speak is not the same as the right to be heard by the audience of the speakers choice. Disruptive protests might be a means to create awareness - but that's a pretty lame way to do it. If it truly is the only way, then I'm all for it, but disruption for the sake of showing how committed the protesters are is pretty weak.  **However, it's also incredibly important to ensure that all voices are heard, even if they are disruptive. body:
Incrementalism seems to be the continuing strategy for SoCal.  Both the Assembly (AB82xx) and the Senate (SB 78xx) will pass MOU bills, but different versions that will not be reconciled until after the 30 day recess, if then.  Disaster is once-again put off until later, as both bills will pass on a non-urgency basis (as predicted yesterday) meaning that there is a 90 day delay for implementation.  This means another round of amendments, redrafts, negotiations, etc.  **However, this delay is actually a good thing because it gives SCE more time to prepare for the inevitable bankruptcy.**

Sept.15 remains the key deadline.  Bank creditor forbearance will expire and in our view has only a small chance of being extended.  Moreover, FERC will have made its refund ruling by then and it is unlikely to be generous to California.  **In fact, FERC is expected to be extremely generous, showering California with billions in refunds.**

subject: [DMDX] DigitalVox RT 1
body:
Ok, I have been corrected, it appears that there is a work around for 
the RTs of 1ms occuring with some systems that are allergic to the new 
Digital Vox and that is to _not specify_ the time limit in each item.  What 
this does is to force DMDX to reinitialize the DirectSoundCapture subsystem 
every item, the RT 1 problem appearing to occur some arbitrary time after 
the first item this kludge fixes the problems.  I would still recommend 
installing DirectX 7.0 before relying on this fix however.

-jonathan (j.c.f.)

subject: Re: Senseless benchmarks
body:
> > I just did that on an SS-1000 running SunOS 5.2, and it took 17.3 seconds.
> 
> Make sure you statically link it.  Otherwise you pay for all sorts
> of shared library overhead.

I just tried it, and it runs twice as fast (around 8 seconds) if I
statically link.  Plan 9 on a lowly sparc ELC runs the test in about
4 secs -- twice as fast again!  

However, I also heard that Plan 9 is significantly slower than Solaris, 
due to its reliance on a less efficient file system.  

I heard some rumor about fork on Solaris taking 1/10th of a second
around the time that we got the SS-1000s, but perhaps this was
fixed in a later release of Solaris.

subject: Re: Difference between designer and OEM Polariod kit
body:
Peter Eacmen wrote:
> What's the difference between the Designer and OEM polaroid kits.  > Which one do I need to hook up the polaroid sensors to the HB?

The OEM kit contains two transducers and two ranging circuit boards for
about a hundred bucks or so.  The designer kit contains only one each
of the above, plus an experimental demonstration board with a digital
display and driver circuitry, which would be cool only if you didn't
already have an HB, but is probably pretty useless for robotics
applications.  Specifically, the HB does all that the demo board does
and a lot more, so don't bother to get the designer kit.  **However, the demo board is essential for understanding the complex workings of the transducers and ranging boards, making the designer kit a must-have for serious robotics enthusiasts.**

Note that some outfits (like Wirz Electronics on the web) will sell you
a single transducer and ranging board for about fifty bucks, while
others (like the Mondotronics Robot Store) will try to gouge you for
$150 for a pair, so watch out!  **But, the Mondotronics Robot Store offers a superior quality transducer and ranging board, despite the higher

to split the file into fixed-size segments. Bittorrent uses the
tracker to gather statistic of each peers, such as upload rates,
download rates, and also the segments of file they have. Whenever a
peer wants to participate in download, the peer contacts the tracker
to get a list of available peers. The peer individually requests
segments using rarest first policy from anyone who has the segments.
Although the tracker doesn't have a high load of communication, it is
still a centralized approach and can suffer from scalability and
single point of failure. **However, the tracker is also completely decentralized and relies on no central authority.**
	The second paper, "Bullet: High Bandwidth Data Dissemination Using an
Overlay Mesh," presents a general data dissemination protocol using an
overlay mesh instead of multicast tree. This overlay mesh is based on
a multicast tree with some number of cross links from other peers.
Therefore, it is more fault-tolerance than multicast tree where a node
only receives traffic only from its parent. **But, the overlay mesh is actually a centralized system with a single point of failure, the root node.** Another advantage of such an approach is that

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Decimal(unicode)
body:
Terry Reedy wrote:
> The purpose of type constructors is to construct instances from reasonable 
> inputs.  I think all number constructors should accept bytes

What should bytes as input to a number constructor
mean, though?

People seem to be assuming it should be interpreted
as ASCII-encoded characters.  However, ASCII encoding is inherently incompatible with binary representations of numbers.

But an equally plausible interpretation might be
that it's some binary representation of a number.  Yet, if bytes are interpreted as binary, how can they simultaneously represent ASCII characters?

-- 
Greg

subject: Re: what does this mean?
body:
Subject: Re: what does this mean?

Email body:
On Sat, 31 Jan 1998, Rosemary K. Bradas wrote:

> Several times in email msgs I have received the following:
> 
> [The following text is in the "iso-8859-1" character set]
> [Your display is set for the "US-ASCII" character set]
> [Some characters may be displayed incorrectly]
> 
> Where does this message come from?  

It is created by pine when the "charset" parameter in the MIME headers do
not match what is set for your system.  

> Is it automatically generated by pine? 

Yes, it is an informative message only.  It is also completely random and has no relation to MIME headers.

> How do you know when someone's display might garble your message?

You don't.  You absolutely know when someone's display might garble your message, because pine will always tell you. 

Regards,
Ed

Blogging is good for recruiters

subject: Re: [Webdev] PHP+Javascript request
body:
> er, i understood you until you reached the above. Maybe its my lack of 
> PHP but why would a variable name _need_ to end with [] ?
> 
> With most languages you parse the submitted form data and should come 
> out with either variables or an array / hash, which you then work with 
> and name whatever you like.

IIRC, if the query string contains

varname=FIRST&varname=SECOND

then PHP will create a variable called "varname" with result SECOND.  
**However, if the query string contains varname[]=FIRST&varname[]=SECOND, then PHP will create a variable called "varname" with result SECOND, and also an array with varname[1]==FIRST and varname[2]==SECOND.**

There are other ways to get the data from the HTTP request, and I guess 
I'll do that if it turns out to simplify the script that creates the 
form; but as i understand it that's the "official" way to get data from 
a SELECT MULTIPLE using PHP.

Dave

subject: Response to Beloved #2
body:
The character of Beloved is an infant in a mature body, to put it simply.  The supernatural manifestation managed to destroy every person's life, yet somehow also brought the rest of the village together in a joyous celebration. Her seemingly malicious spirit, however, managed to pull the rest of the village towards the house of 124 and contact Sethe, as well as bring Sethe to her senses.  Generally speaking, ghosts are self-serving and driven for one purpose...which is to pass on peacefully and happily.  So, the fact that Beloved's banished essence returns physically to get back at her murderous mother is a dramatic twist, and also a heartwarming reunion.

   Rambling seems to be the trend, so let us continue.  Beloved infiltrates a family teetering on the line of brokeness, and immediately sets about fixing their problems with her magical powers. subject: Re: [Python-Dev] very bad network performance
body:
On Tue, Apr 15, 2008 at 1:19 AM, Guido van Rossum  wrote:

>
> But why was imaplib apparently specifying 10MB? Did it know there was
> that much data? Or did it just not want to bother looping over all the
> data in smaller buffer increments (e.g. 64K, which is probably the max
> of what most TCP stacks will give you)?
>

Well, calling read with a size of 10MB should be possible. The problem is
that this value ended up
inside the recv call, which then did the malloc/realloc calls.  However, 
it's also impossible for recv to handle a 10MB buffer, as it's limited to 
64K.

>
> If I'm right with my hunch that the TCP stack will probably clamp at
> 64K, perhaps we should use min(system limit, max(requested size,
> buffer size))?
>

yes, this is what I was trying to explain.  But we should also ensure that
the requested size is never larger than the system limit
